[JS Weird World] No.32 A Framework Reminder: Function Overloading
JsWeirdWorld
JavaScript 全攻略:克服JS奇怪的部分

Here's something kind of special — a feature that exists in plenty of other languages but not in JavaScript: function overloading. In this post I'll walk through what overloading is, why JavaScript doesn't have it, and the patterns we use instead, like default values and ES6 default parameters.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/19/js-weird-world-31-arguments-and-rest-parameters/
[JS Weird World] No.31 arguments and the Rest Parameter (spread)
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts

Time for another special keyword in JavaScript — one that gets set up automatically every time you run a function: `arguments`. We'll also look at ES6's rest parameter (often written with the spread `...` syntax). You'll see what `arguments` actually holds, why it's an array-like object, and how the rest parameter helps when you don't know how many arguments are coming in.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/19/jsweirdworld-30-arrays-hold-anything/
[JS Weird World] No.30 Quick Reminder: An Array Can Hold Literally Anything
JavaScript Full Guide: Conquering the Weird Parts of JS

In a lot of languages an array can only hold one type of thing. JavaScript doesn't care — you can mix numbers, strings, booleans, objects, even functions in the same array. Here's a quick reminder of why that works, plus how to call a function that's sitting inside an array (and pass it another array element as an argument).

[JS Weird World] No.29 Objects, Functions, and this
JavaScript Full Guide: Beating the Weird Parts of JS

Time to revisit the execution context we touched on at the start, and finally cover the one thing I kept skipping: this. We'll walk through where this points in different situations — global scope, anonymous functions, object methods — and the classic "this got lost inside a nested function" gotcha that trips up almost everyone.

[JS Weird World] No.28 A Quick Reminder: Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference

We're going to dig into one of the things you'll bump into constantly in JavaScript — pass by value and pass by reference. It's a really important concept, and if it's fuzzy in your head, it'll come back to bite you later during development and debugging. So let's clear it up once and for all.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/18/js-weird-world-27-function-statements-vs-expressions/
[JS Weird World] No.27 Function Statements vs Function Expressions

Last chapter we figured out that functions are just objects. Now we're going to use that idea to do some genuinely fun and powerful stuff. But before we get there, we need to understand the difference between function statements and function expressions — and why one gets hoisted and the other doesn't.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/18/js-weird-world-26-functions-are-objects/
[JS Weird World] No.26 Functions Are Just Objects

"Functions are objects" — that title probably leaves you a little confused, and that's fair. Let's go back to one of the most basic, most fundamental concepts in JavaScript. Understanding it is what lets you go beyond just using JavaScript and start treating it as a tool other languages can't quite match. The concept is called first-class functions.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/18/js-weird-world-25-json-and-object-literals/
[JS Weird World] No.25 JSON and Object Literals — They're Closer Than You Think

A lot of people misunderstand what JSON actually is. So what is it? JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation, and it was inspired by JavaScript object literals — when you write an object literal, you're basically writing something that looks a lot like JSON. In this post we'll clear up the common confusion and cover JSON.stringify() and JSON.parse().
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/18/jsweirdworld-24-faking-namespaces-with-objects/
[JS Weird World] No.24 A Framework Tip: Faking Namespaces in JavaScript

In most modern languages a namespace is a container for variables and functions — it keeps names separate so that A doesn't bleed into B. JavaScript doesn't actually have namespaces, but here's the trick: you can use plain objects to fake them. This post walks through how that works, and the one gotcha that throws a fatal error.
/en/jsweirdworld/2019/05/17/jsweirdworld-23-object-literals/
[JS Weird World] No.23 Objects and Object Literals

Time for another way to build objects. Remember how we used `new Object();` last time? Honestly, that approach feels pretty unnatural to write. So in this post we look at object literal syntax — just curly braces — which is way easier to type and a lot nicer to read.
